modes.name: dir
modes.summary: Directory and file brute-force against an HTTP/S target. The most common mode — find admin panels, hidden APIs, leftover backups.

modes.name: dns
modes.summary: Subdomain brute-force via DNS resolution. No HTTP requests — just A/AAAA queries against a wordlist of names.

modes.name: vhost
modes.summary: Virtual-host brute-force on a single IP. Differs from dns: tests Host headers against the same IP, finding sites that don't have public DNS.

modes.name: fuzz
modes.summary: Generic placeholder substitution. Replace `FUZZ` anywhere in the URL or body — for parameter mining, GET-value enumeration, etc.

modes.name: s3
modes.summary: AWS S3 bucket discovery via wordlist of bucket names.

modes.name: gcs
modes.summary: Google Cloud Storage bucket discovery — same idea as s3 mode, against GCS naming.

modes.name: tftp
modes.summary: TFTP file enumeration. Niche but useful against IoT / embedded targets.

commonFlags.description: Wordlist file. Required for every mode.
Concurrent threads. Default 10; 50-100 is fine on most targets.
Skip TLS certificate verification — labs / self-signed certs.
Comma-separated extensions to append: -x php,html,bak,txt (dir mode).
Status codes treated as hits. Default `200,204,301,302,307,401,403`.
Status codes to ignore (blacklist) instead of -s.
Skip responses of exactly N bytes — kills wildcard / 200-on-everything noise.
Send a Cookie header — for authenticated content discovery.
Override User-Agent. Useful for masquerading as a browser or mobile UA.
Write results to FILE. Pair with `-q` to suppress the progress chatter.
Hide network / DNS errors from the output.
Force-continue against wildcard responses (rare; you usually want the default behaviour).
vhost mode: append the base domain to each wordlist entry.

wordlists.purpose: Default starting point for dir mode. ~30 k entries, broad coverage.
Larger raft — slower but catches more. Run after the medium list.
Quick first pass — ~4 k common names. Good for time-boxed CTFs.
Default DNS mode wordlist — top 5 k subdomains.
Aggressive subdomain enumeration. Hours-long runs against busy DNS.
Classic dirb list (~20 k). Older and noisier than raft, but well-known.

recipes.title: Quick directory scan with extensions
recipes.details: First pass against most web apps. -k for self-signed labs; the extension list catches `index.php`, `backup.bak`, plain `robots.txt`.

recipes.title: Subdomain enumeration
recipes.details: DNS resolution only — no HTTP. Pipe `subs.txt` into a follow-up dir scan. Watch for wildcard DNS that resolves everything.

recipes.title: Vhost discovery on a single IP
recipes.details: Useful when DNS doesn't reveal everything (internal apps, dev hosts). Each wordlist entry becomes a Host header.

recipes.title: Filter out a wildcard response by length
recipes.details: When the app returns 200 for everything, find the size of a known-bad path and exclude it. Real hits are different sizes.

recipes.title: Authenticated content discovery
recipes.command:
gobuster dir -u https://target/ -w big.txt \
  -c "session=abc123; csrf=xyz" \
  -H "X-CSRF-Token: xyz" \
  -t 30
recipes.details: Reuse the cookies / headers from a logged-in browser. Capture them in Burp and paste into -c / -H.

recipes.title: Status-code filtering
recipes.details: Whitelist the codes that mean something interesting; blacklist the noise. -s and -b are mutually exclusive — use one.

recipes.title: Parameter fuzzing with FUZZ
recipes.details: fuzz mode swaps the placeholder with each wordlist entry. Combine with response-size filtering to spot endpoints that react to specific values.

tips: Always set -k against lab targets and CTFs — the cert is almost always self-signed.
Layer the scans: raft-medium first, then raft-large. Don't start with the largest wordlist.
If you get 200 OK for every path, the app has a wildcard handler. Use --exclude-length with the size of a known-bad URL to filter it out.
For dir mode, -x is essential: many apps only respond to /admin.php, never /admin. Add the platform's natural extensions.
Use -o on every run. The terminal scrolls fast; the output file is your evidence trail.
Subdomain wildcards (*.example.com) make DNS mode return everything. Check with `dig` first; if wildcards are configured, vhost mode is more useful than dns.
Pair Gobuster with a slower follow-up tool: ffuf for response-size diffing, feroxbuster for recursive walks. Gobuster is best at the broad first pass.
limitations: Brute force only — only finds paths / names that appear in the wordlist. Quality of results scales directly with wordlist quality.
Loud by design. WAFs and naive rate-limiters catch the traffic pattern; production targets will block the source IP quickly.
vhost mode false-positives when the server returns the same response for unknown hosts (default backend). Verify with response-size filtering.
DNS mode has no HTTP visibility — a resolved name doesn't mean a working web app. Always follow up with a dir scan.
Testing applications you do not have written authorisation to test is illegal in most jurisdictions.
